CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The eloquent Mr. Birrell has applied the balm of
oratory to some of Ireland’s wounds. He said some-
thing pretty about the sale of a nation recently. But
what about the fees for Irish; no one knows better than
the people of this country that talk is cheap. The
eloquent Mr. Birrell, in the course of a reply to Mr.
Thomas O’Donnell, said :—“The Commissioners of Na-
tional Education being of the opinion that the scale of
fees, which I have just mentioned, was insufficient for
the remuneration of extern teachers made proposals of
an increase in the scale in connection with the Estimates
for 1906-7, and these proposals were supported by the
Irish Government. The Treasury, however, felt un-
able to sanction these proposals, and my hon. friend,
the Secretary to that Department, has already informed
the hon. member.”

Now, what in the name of common sense is this scape-
goat by the name of the Treasury? The National Com-
missioners and the Irish Government want extra fees
for Irish, but the eloquent Mr. Birrell tells us that
“The Treasury, however, felt unable to sanction these
proposals.” Indeed! Well, why does not the
“ Tiberal ’ Government compel “ the Treasury,” what-
ever that is, to feel able, whether it likes or not, to
provide a few paltry pounds sterling further for the

teaching of Irish? What is the use of being harangued

about Ireland being governed according to Irish ideas

if “the Treasury ’ can refuse increased fees for Irish

merely because it is in the mood of feeling “ unable to

sanction these proposals?’ Supposing the Government

deal with the University grievance, perhaps,  the Trea-

swry *’ may take it into its head to “feel unable’’ to~
sanction the Government’s proposals!

By reference to the Directory we find that “ The
Treasury, Whitehall, 8.W.,”” is described as an institu-
tion *for conducting the receipt and expenditure ‘of
His Majesty’s Revenue.””  Its Lords Commissioners
appear to be (probably there have been changes since,
for the Directory before us is for last year), Campbell-
Bannerman, First Lord; H. H. Asquith, Chancellor of
the Exchequer, and Messrs. J. A. Pease, M.P.; Her-
bert Lewis, M.P. ; Freeman F. Thomas, M.P., and Cap-
tain C. W. Norton, M.P. Then follow the names of
some secretaries and a lot of clerks.

Now, when the “Eloquent Dempsey,”” or rather, as
we should have said, the eloquent Mr. Birrell, states
that “the Treasury, however, felt unable to sanction
these proposals,”’ did he mean to convey that “ the
Treasury ’’ is some sort of thing independent of Par-
liament, and before the whims of which the “ Liberal *’
Party must bow? Surely Sir Campbell-Bannerman and
Mr. Asquith, and the other M.P.’s who are on or in
“the Treasury,”’ have a voice in the matter. ~Why
does not Campbell-Bannerman and Mr. Asquith, of the
Treasury, exert themselves with a view to getting these
extra fees that both the National Commissioners and the
Irish Government, whatever that is, have asked for?

It would appear to us that the Irish Party would be
allowing the “ Liberal *’ Government and the eloquent
Mr. Birrell to humbug them before their eyes if they
lie down before such an excuse as “the Treasury, how-
ever, felt unable to sanction these proposals.”’ = Why
doesn’t Mr. John Redmond move the adjournment of
the House, or take whatever other course that may be
practicable in order to press this matter of Irish fees
and induce the “ Liberal’’ Government to induce its
Sir Campbell-Bannerman, Mr. Asquith and others of
“the Treasury,”’ to sit up? We see no case at present
for the withdrawal of the Irish Party from the British
Parliament, but whilst they are there they ought to fight
for the fees for Irish, and not be put off by the elo-
quence of Mr. Birrell. If they allow themselves to be
rocked into sweet slumber by soft talk, we fear they
will have a rude awakening in store for them, both
from the “Liberal’’ Government and from their con-
stituents at home.

We have received the following sums for the Lan-
guage Fund :—Father T. O’Meara, St. Oswald’s, Ash
ton-in-Makerfield, Lancs., £1; Subscriber, bs. Fathe
0O’Meara writes— March 13, 1907.—Dear Mr. Moran—
May I trouble you to take charge of the enclosed
cheque (£1) for the Language Fund? It is my way of
acknowledging your invaluable services to Irish Ireland.
—Yours sincerely, J. O’Meara."

In the course of his letter “ Subscriber’’ says:—
“ Enclosed is 5s. subscription to the Language Fund.
I wish it forwarded through the LEADER so as to mark
our appreciation of the' grand work it has done for
Irish TIreland. I owe it to the Leapkr only
that I am a Gaelic Leaguer, and that I know more than
one energetic Gaelic Leaguer of the verb ‘to do’ type
who have the same story to tell.”’ 5 4

There are certainly several bitter bigots amongst the
members of the (Clity of London Union. Father
Bohrer applied for the use of the dining hall at the

The

Work]gou&e for purposes of Catholic™ worship.
Committee recommended  that permission be given to
the Rev. T. Bohrer to hold services in the dining hall
On & convenient day in each week at 6.30 p.m., on con-
dition that he shall undertake to have signed the tic-
kets to be given to Roman Catholic inmates attending
outside Sunday services.”” The Rev. Prebendary Ing-
ram and the two other Church of England clergymen
present, supported the recommendation, but there were
true blues who would have none of it. A Mr. M. Mann
Cross, according to a report in the Lonmdon “ Daily
Telegyaph,” strongly opposed the motion, remarking
that it was a bad principle to set up rival religions in
the house! We suppose the good principle would be
that all the inmates should take their views on re-
ligion from Mr. M. Mann Cross, whoever he may be.
“I am a bigot,”” declared a Mr. R. Evans, and he pro-
tested against such privileges being given to the unfor-
tunate Catholic inmates. The Chairman of the Board,
a Mr. J. B. Wild, supported Mr. Mann Cross, and
said that his only regret was that they ever let the
Roman Catholic priest into the house at all. We be-
11e}'0 that this tolerant Mr. J. B. Wild is proprietor of
Wild’s Temperance Hotel, in Ludgate Hill, London.
We wonder does Mr. Wild object to letting Catholic
priests and Catholic lay visitors to London from Ire-
land or elsewhere into his hotel? The recommenda-
tion of the Committee was defeated by fourteen votes to
eleven. There’s tolerance for you! We particularly
take note of this instance of gross intolerance in Lon-
don, as no doubt a good many of the unfortunate
Catholic inmates of this workhouse are Irish by birth
or extraction.

The organ of Mr. Martyn’s Party has failed to pub-
lish a well-reasoned and temperate letter criticising
itself and some of the prominent men associated with
that party. We published the letter, but none of the
men impeached has replied in our columns. Some up-
holders of this party of cranks without a leader, who
were anxious to hand North Tyrone over to the Orange
bigots, are apparently, and very justly, sore over all
this matter. We have received two letters concerning
the affair from unauthorised outsiders, and we have pub-
lished them. Both were written over pen-names. We
published the first as a concession, for we did not, and
do nct, admit the right of the correspondent to interfere
when the principals—we must not use the word
“leaders ’—remain silent. However, we told the
second Richmond, who had a mind to stroll into the
field where his “ principals’’ were too wary to tread,
that we would, as a concession, print his epistle if he
put his name and address to it. We must draw the
line somewhere. If the “ principals ”’ have anything to
say our columns are free to them, but we cannot pro-
mise to put in everything, even though it is sent over
a name and address, that unauthorised persons may send
along to us. As we write we have not received any
further letters on the matter, but Seazan s Neactan
is replying to  €amonn Mac¢3ov of 32 Richmond
Place, N. C. Road, Dublin.

We received a letter from Cork from a correspondent
who, with a candour that does him credit, describes him-
self in the course of his letter as “an ignorant Irish
Catholic.”” It is certainly part of our purpose that
the LEapEr should be read by the ignorant with a view
to their attaining to some share of enlightenment, but
it is not our purpose that ignorant people should con-
tribute to our columns. This “ignorant Irish Catholic’
asks some questions concerning the Catholic Church and
politics; he also asks what is the policy of the LmAper.
We did not insert his letter in our last issue, and so on

THE LEADER.

MARcH 23, 1907.

TFriday we received another letter from this-“ignorant
Trish Catholic,”” which takes the shape of an inquiry
and an ultimatum. Wo sent him a reply by post, of
which the following is a copy:—

“A Capa~——In reply to your letter of the 14th inst.,
inquiring about your previous one, I think your best
course would be to consult your confessor on questions
that may trouble your conscience as ‘an ignorant Trish
Catholic.” The LEApER is not a Catholic paper in the
theological sense, and its province is not the cure of
souls ; it is meroly a paper edited by a lay Catholic who
makes no pretensions to be a subtle theologian, and who
labours under no hallucination that he has a mission to
reform the Church, or the Hierarchy, in Ireland or
clsewhere. Its contributors are not necessarily Catho-
lics, and sometimes are non-Catholics. As to the p.ohcy
of the Leaper, I think your best cotrse as ‘an igno-
rant Trish Catholic’ would be to carefully read over its
files. It was started in the latter half of the year 1900.
However, if I may presume that you have been a con-
stant reader of it, and have failed Yo understand its
policy, would it not be futile for me to attempt to
explain to ‘an ignorant Irish Catholic’ in a paragraph
or two what he apparently has failed to gather from
a perusal of upwards of 440 complete issues? 1
promise you, if you have your letter published in any
other paper—the ‘Cork Examiner’ for choice—that I
will lift it into my columns.”

Now we think that is not only quite fair, bub
generous. As soon as our correspondent induces any
other paper {o publish his letter we shall oblige him by
letting him feast his .eyes on it “in print " in the
LEADER.

It is a pity to have the language movement mixed up
with gross raimeis. The “ Freeman’’ had a leading
article last Thursday on “ Irish Language Week.”
Probably someone of the staff, who is in the sixth baoik
of O’Growney, was ‘turned on to this work! Here is a
choice specimen—* Wherever a supporter of the lan-
guage movement is to be found—and that means al
this day, wherever there beats a heart in which runs
a drop of Irish blood,’’ etc. The italics are ours. Surely
the “ Freeman '’ might keep the writer of this sort of
stuff in exclusive reserve for writing up “magnificent
meetings ’’ and drafting pulverising resolutions; it is
pure irony to order this young man to write on, of all
things in the world, a “language *’ movement!

Mr. D. A. Spillane, Mr. P. Huggard, and Mr. T. T.
O’Connor, of the Killarney Urban Council, have not
Dbeen left without an answer from the local branch of the
Gaelic League. It will be remembered that Mr. Hug-
gard, referring to a resolution r¢ Humphrey’s Dump,
which had been received from the Gaelic League, re-
marked—* Don’t take any notice of it,”” and Mr. Spil-
lane said—“ Read. Waste-paper basket for it now.”
At a special meeting of the Killarney Branch of the
Gaelic League, these gentlemen were severely censured,
and a resolution pledging the members to use their in-
fluence “to secure more fitting representatives on the
Urban Council at the next election ’’ was unanimously
passed.

A. Belfast" correspondent infoims us that in a boot
establishment in one of the leading streets of the city
of the foul-smelling abattoir there is displayed what

with wolf dog, ete. Tt is also decorated with the legend

at a translation into Trish as follows:—" @ipeacgac
Fion €imeaxzae.””  Our correspondent tells us that he
could not see any manufacturer’s name on it, and he

Perhaps some
of our readers could tell us something of this polish and
of its maker.

It is rather late in the day to take notice of “ Freddy
Trenches’ Budget’’ of December last, but it was only on
Saturday that we received a copy of it from @ friend.
This budget of scrapings, officially called “Gricvanses
from Ir(;lancl,” is run in connection with the bigoted
corporation called the “Imperial Protestant Federa-
tion.” Tord Ashtown’s scrapings of Decembor last
d_evotes two pages to the Gnelic League, and these prin-
cipally are taken up with a reported speech by Mr.
Fionan MacColuim, chief organiser for Munster. One
of the parts of Mr. MacColuim’s speech that is printed
1u extra heavy black type, reads as follows:—. . . .,
and if the language is allowed to go down, we will have
losb_ our best claim to recognition as a separate
nadionality, .. . . .” Dreadful, isn't it!
Quite enough to put my Lord Ashtown into
hysterics. Cardinal Logue also is held up in extra
heavy black type for declaring that “he trusted that
the movement would be crowned by what most of them
desired—a real control over their affairs in Ireland.”

Bangor, Co. Down, known to readers of Irish history
as the seat of oune of the most famous scats of learning
and religion in the days of Ireland’s ancient culture.
At present, it is notable for the rapidity of its growth
as a stucco villa suburb of Belfast, and the favourite
resort of the average business class sea-side visitor. In
July, the Glasgow-fair tripper is predominant. Ban-
gor’s ancient fame is largely forgotten. Tt is pleasant
to note in the report of a Presbyterian Literary Guild
meeting, that “ Ireland’s Ancient Schools and Scholars’
was the subject of a lecture by Mrs. F. H. G. Wilson,
who illuminated the memories of Colmecille, Columbanus
and Comgall, for many who were beforetime ignorant.
The lecture, printed to the extent of several columns in
the local county paper, will find a wider audience than
the Presbyterian Guild. Irish step-dancing has also
been popularised in Bangor this season, as at several
entertainments the services of the Lisnadeane Gaelic
League dancers from Belfast were enlisted. And Mr.
Fred Hughes, who has recently become a Bangor resi-
dent, has lost no opportunity of keeping gennine Irish
folk-music to the front.

There was a debate at the Tullamore Gaelic League
on “The Sinn Fein Policy versus Parliamentarianism,’”
an excellent report of which appears in the ¢ Westmeath
Independent.” Mr. Michael O’Sullivan, who was the
chief pioneer of the Gaclic League in Wexford, in the
course of his remarks, said:—" The Sinn Fein policy
was calculated to divorce or wean sympathy from the
Gaelic League movement. That was his objection to
the Sinn Fein policy, which cannot possibly succeed in
the present generation.”” Mr, O’Sullivan’s opinion on
auy matbter pertaining to the Gaelic League, for which
he has done such great work, is deserving of attention ;
and anything that is calculated to divorce or wean sym-
pathy from the Gaelic League at the present juncture,
when the Gaelic League is none too strong, calls like-
wise for serious consideration.

A note in our own paper was the only reference we
saw this year concerning the shutting of the public
houses of Dublin on. St. Patrick’s Day. As St. Pat-
rick’s Day fell on Sunday this year, the publicans

THE LEADER. ; 67

might have acted as patriots by shutting for the threg
hours which British Law allows them to remain open.
We cannot say how many, if any, did shut up, but
we noticed a great many open. We have not heard
that any public house in Dublin closed on Monday, the
bank holiday.  Mooney’s - opened, and Mr. Gerald
Mooney, managing-director of Mooney’s, is one of the
three directors of the “truly national’” “Freeman’’!
‘We hear nothing now of the Bill to bung up Bung on
St. Patrick’s Day; and until such time as we have
legislative power ourselves, there is no force but the
force of an Act of the British Pariiament that will
turn the publicans into patriots on St. Patrick’s Day.

The “Irish Times’’ that gave eleven lines to the
Catholic Demonstration on Sunday, published over a
column of a report of the first annual dinner of the
new mystery that calls itself Companions of 8t.
Patrick.”” The “companions’’ dined on Saturday
evening under the “tame’’ gaze,of its president, the
“tame’’ Catholic, Mr. James Talbot Power. Mr.
James Crozier, J.P., the Freemason, was there.
Amongst the “tried and true ’’ patriots at this “ God-
Save-the-King ”’ dinner were Mr. J. G. MacSweeny
and Mr. J. M. Briscoe, T.C., the Town Tenants’ man.
Mr. J. T. Power proposed “ The King’’ at this delect-
able party, and we are told “the toast was honoured
by the singing of ¢ God Save the King.’”” We wonder
is Mr. Maw Coghlan Briscoe the possessor of a rich
tenor or a deep bass? And what of the vocal powers
of Mr. J. G. MacSweeney?. The Lord Mayor apolo-
gised for his absence owing to illness, which we all
regret, and amongst the others who apologised was the
Grand Steward of the Freemasons, Brother Goulding,
the author, if our recollection serves us well, of the
famous phrase, “England’s Faithful Garrison.” A
pretty companion of St. Patrick he!  What is the
game of the self-styled  Companions of St. Patrick,”
and why did the “ Irish Times,’’ that gave eleven lines
all told to the Catholic Demonstraticn on Sunday, give
nearly a column and a quarter to the first dinner of
this corporation?

The Irish language appears to have been well in
evidence in the Churches in honour of St. Patrick this
year. In Dublin there were Irish sermons in the Pro-
Cathedral, and in St. Joseph's, Berkeley Road; at
Mount Argus the Rosary was recited in Irish. Belfast
this year gave a lead to Dublin, as previously London
—at least, Irish Ireland in London—gave a lead to Ire-
land.  Rev. Dr. O’Hickey, Maynooth, preached in
Irish in the Clathedral, Waterford, and in Ballina
Rev. Father Munnelly delivered an Irish sermon.
At the children’s Mass at the (Cathedral in
Cork, over 2,000 children of the North Parish Schools
attended, and a sermon in Irish was preached by
Father Fitzgerald. There were hymns and prayers in
Trish, but the latter were no novelty, for there are
Trish hymns and prayers at this particular Mass at the
Cathedral, Cork, all the year round, for Father Fitz.
gerald is in charge of this service.

To-night (Thursday) and also on Friday and Satur-
day nights, a new play in three Acts, “The Fiddler’s
House,”’ by Padraic Colum, will be staged in the large
Concert Room, Rotunda, under the auspices of
clueéeonni na h-Gipeann (The Theatre of Ireland). It
will be preceded each evening by “The Last Feast of

It was' only this week that we saw a copy of the
February number of “ The Arrow,” a long haired sort
of a newspaper issued occasionally from the home of
“Art,” in Abbey Street, where the police, under the
instruction of Mr. W. B. Yeats, made short work of
men who dared to hiss at the “ comedy’’ about the
man who “XKilled his da.”” We notice in heavy type
the following concise heading to an extract from the
LEADER :—“ On Mr. Boyle and Mr. Synge, and whebher
the Liverpool Irish would hiss them from the stage.’’
Pretty, isn’t it? We wonder what primeval dialect is
that heading written in! There is a sub-heading:
‘““Article in TrE LEADER, December 15th, 1906, signed
J. Bull.”” Then follows, not an article, but an extract
from an anticle or rather skit which appeared in our
columns.  The skit from which the extract is taken
was in the form of a letter supposed to have been
written by a born Britisher to a friend in England.
The letter is in the English-as-she-is-spoken dialect.
Of course any reader of “The Arrow’ who does not
read the LEADER—if one such person exists—is not in-
formed that the article, signed “J. Bull,” is a skit
that contained some delightful references—not quoted
—+to “a chap called Yeats.”” Whatever may be said
of Mr. Yeats as a writer of prose, there is no doubt
about it he is a tricky fellow. Now, if we in the course
of an ironical article made the ironical remark, “Mr.

The Belfast Tailoring House, known in trade as The
Tailors’ Co-Pantnership, wihose partiality for Irish-
mado goods have made them deservedly popular with
Irish Irolanders, have ghanged their address in Belfast.
Their new premises, which are more suitable angl cen-
tral for their business, are situated at 10+ Garfield
Street, next door to Avenue Hall. B

In another part of this paper Messrs. Getz and Co.,
Waterford, invite lovers of music to procure copies
of Chief O’Neill’s “ Music of Ireland,” which may be
had direct from this firm, bound in green and gold
cloth, for 13s. 7d. each post free. ~Many of our
rcaders will recall that Chief O’Neill’s book was intro-
duced to this country under the auspices of bhat_welL
known Irish-Treland priest, Father Fielding of Chicago.

Mr. T. O’Gorman, Outfitter, Cork, whose announce-
ments in Tue LeapEr have made him familiar to our
readers, is able to record an important expansion in
the Cap-making Industry, which he established some
cighteen months ago. Mr.  O’Gorman commenced in
the cap line at 7 Mulgrove Road, Cork, and we are
glad to say that in the interval his business has grown
altogether too big for the original premises. He has now
fitted up larger premises at 16 Rope’s Quay, Cork,
where the industry will in future be located. We wish
Mr. O’Gorman further success in his enterprise.

Thousands of people will regret that the relatives of
John O’Leary did not see their way to permit the hold-
ing of a public funeral. = The hearts'of the TIrish
people in every land, whatever their shade of Na-
tionalist views may be, will do homage in spirit to his
memory, and breathe a prayer for the soul of the dead
Fenian.

SUNDAY'S CATHOLIC DEMONSTRATION.

\ HE Catholic demonstration in the streets of
Dublin on Sunday last surprised everybody. It
was one of the largest processions that we ever wit-
nessed in Dublin. The idea was suggested in
the “ Irish Rosary’’ of this month, and many doubted
whether it would be wise to risk a failure by attempt-
ing to work ouf the suggestion. It was only within a
few days of Sunday last that the first preliminary meet-
ing was held, and we wrote in last week’s issue:—
“There is not adequate time since the enterprise was
initiated to make elaborate preparations, so that the
demonstration must be, to a great extent, of what we
may call a spontaneous character. This is all the more
reason why our Dublin Catholic readers should exert
themselves enthusiastically in the short time interven-
ing with a view to making the demonstration an honour
to the Catholic spirit of Dublin.”” TUnder the circum-
stances we should have considered the demonstration a
success if it had been only one-fifth the size of last Sun-
day’s magnificent parade and protest. The very short
time in which the demonstration was organised was not
the only exceptional fact that lent emphasis to its magni-
tude; there was very little help given to it by the daily
so-called Catholic Press. We assume that the organisers
deemed it desirable to spend some money on big adver-
tisements in the Kopje-Climber of Prince’s Street and
its little brother, the “Evening Telegraph,” with a
view to getting these papers to write up the demonstra-
tion to some extent. Mr. Murphy’s “ Green ’’ of Satur-
day had scarcely anything about the procession—a few
lines only. But then, as far as we saw, it got no
advertisement, and how could Mr. Murphy’s * Green #
be expected to run the risk of frightening some Sour-
face ha’pennies by writing up the procession unless, at
least, it got some large advertisements beforehand !
However, the daily papers walked very much behind in
this great procession, this almost spontaneous outburst
of Catholic feeling.

It was, above all else, a Catholic procession, a,nd‘ t_m‘d
no incongruous features. Confraternities, sodaht.leg,
Catholic Young Men’s Societies, and so forth, assembled
as Irish Catholics to protest against the robbery and
spoliation of the Church in France. And the Church in
Trance and the Church in Ireland are tperely parts of
the Universal Church, and the Church in any country
in the world has an interest in the Church in every
other country. .

Without speculating as to what effect, if any, it may
have on the infidel Government of France, let us say
that the demonstration was badly wanted in Ireland.
No doubt by this.time it has given many people pause.
It has shown the pol;eutia,lity of the Catholic spirit of
Dublin, and through Dublin ' of Ireland. As we
watched the procession marching past, we thought what

‘not go badly.

o

& Pity it is that the force and determination which it
ra;lresented. is not frequently focussed on Catholic
grievances in Ireland. A demonstration against out-
Tages on Catholics in France is good; a demonstration

: _against outrages on Catholic rights in Ireland would be

even better ; 1t would, to be sure, call for more pluck and
determination, more readiness to accept the conse-
quences of fair and just fight. It would get up the
backs of the bigots, and tend to tighten the grip of the
Freemasons; the anti-Catholic bigots in TIreland will
not be pleased with Sunday’s great Catholic Demon-
stration, but they can afford to keep cool over Irish
Catholic indignation against spoliation—in France;
what if the Catholics demonstrated against spoliation
and _b)got;ry at home. At home we Catholics are largely
despised by our encmies because we show timidity in
the fighting line.

The demonstration will, we believe, have the effect
of putting some more badly needed spirit into the
Catholic laity, for it is encouraging to cvery individual
who, as an individual, feels his weakness to have
ocular proof of the strength of the organisation to
which he belongs. The demonstration of Sunday was

. something that the Irish Times’’ might bitterly hate,

but which it would not have the temerity to sneer at.
‘We looked in vain through the ““Irish Times’’ of Mon-
day for even one line of reference to Sunday’s great
demonstration; the “Irish Times’ treated it in fear
and trembling, to the eloquence of silence.  (This
article had gone to the printers when a friend discovered
two brief references to the demonstration in the “ Irish
Times.”” The two references total up to eleven lines!
May we describe them as silence emphasised?)

It would be quite possible to drug the Catholic
opinion of this country into a dangerous half-sleep.
We doubt if it has not, or had not, been partially done
already, and as a corrective it is well that the Catholics
should shake themselves and publicly demonstrate as
Catholics on some Catholic issue in the public thorough-
fares for all men to see. A few coteries or a handful
of unconnected individuals by throwing about mud,
and doubts and sneers, and likewise many Catholics of
weak faith in the faith of the people, might, as a result
of their combined efforts, spread a dangerous feeling
amongst individual Catholics that the Catholic faith in
Ireland was soft and honeycombed with internal weak-
ness, and such views, if they spread, would tend
to bring about a deplorable state of affairs in the

general body. The very fact that many people
feared failure for what turned out to be a
demonstration beyond the dreams of the san-

guine showed that their ‘estimate of the Catholics
of Dublin in connection with a demonstration of this
kind was very erroneous. All nationalities can flourish
within the folds of the Catholic Church, and those
Catholics make the best Irishmen who stand manfully
before the world by the faith of Patrick as did the
great multitude that marched through the streets of
Dublin on Sunday last. Fan Cjerom gan Tip.

her marriage to the family weal. But her strong
sonse of filial duty upheld her, and she went to the
sacrifice with dogged determination, 7
The husand chosen for her was old enough to be her
father ; possessed scant grace of manner or person; and,
‘worst of all, was a hard drinker, and fond of gambling.
But just because he was socially and financially in a
better position than they, and was willing to take their
handsome young daughter without a penny, the parents
daty little amiss in the match—there were many mouths
%o feed in their none-too-prosperous household, other
daughters to portion off, young sons to start in life.
Qo the marriage took place, and for a time things did

The novelty of a young and very pretty wife kept
fh'e'elderly husband fairly steady f91‘ a time; but, alas!

firmed drunkatd, and it was soon plain that even his
ample means could not long stand the strain of his reck-
less gambling. Deep and bitter was the humiliation cf
the proud and reticent young wife in those days; small
wonder if her face grew hard, her manner even more
grave, and that she began to look on life with a
lethargic and hopeless eye. i

But a day came when her heart woke up with a throb
of bliss; when hope tingled fresh and joyous, and the
tenderness and glory of motherhood enveloped her, and
blotted out the painful past. From that moment of
unspeakable delight when she first held her baby boy in
her arms, her whole nature was transformed. How
precious and how dear he was! Here, indeed, was
something to live for! She even began to hope under
the softening influence of this new cxperience that the
baby might succeed where she had failed, and win the
father to a steadier and better mode of life. But the
latter was too confirmed a rake to be drawn on to tle
right path by even those autocratic baby fingers.  He
was by this time in very poor health, brought on by his
own excesses, and he was laid beside his forbears in the
green churchyard long before the boy’s second birth-
day.

The young widow did not pretend a grief; she could
not feel, and people began to shake their heads and opine
that before long old Hussey’s place would be filled.
Many a man would have “hopped’’ at the comely
widow and the fine old place, encumbered though this
last must” have been by its late owner’s extravagance.
But suitors got nothing for their pains but a dignified
and impassable indifference. Before long it was seen
that the widow IHussey had no thought of re-marrying
—her whole heart was centred in her boy. And how
lovable he was by this! A little fair-haired, blue-eyed
lad who made the sunshine of his mother’s life. ~ And
at first that life was hard enough in all conscience. She
was practically penniless at her husband’s death, and
deeply in debt on his behalf.  She could never have
fought the terrible army of difficulties but for the boy.
As it was, she faced them with the dogged determina-
tion which she displayed when she first came to the
farm a dutiful, if reluctant bride. How she did it no
one knew ; but as time went by, she not only managed
to keep the farm, and pay her husband’s debts, but
began to be rated as a woman of wealth and considera-
tion. No woman ever worked harder; summer dawns
found her already busy; and every soul in the house-
hold was enjoying well-earned rest, hours before the
busy hands and capable brain of the mistress had ceased
their work. The little lad was her consolation ; at the
touch of his-velvet cheek on hers, the closing of his
little fingers on her own, all the heavy burden of life .
was lifted from her shoulders, and the rough places
were smoothed and made beautiful. And, if she did
spoil him somewhat, who can blame her? He was her
one treasure, her one compensation, and all the pent-up
love of her big passionate heart was lavished upon him.
As he grew older, and the baby frocks were replaced
by suits, it was just the same: the hardest day’s toil
was nothing to the mother who welcomed her little
school-boy home, and listened patiently and understand-
ingly to all the doings of his little day.

Time passed with no laggard feet, and brought his
twenty-first birthday before the mother could realise it.
But how happy and content she was! What a pro-
sperous future she had planned out for her darling!
Thanks to her hard work, he was comparatively wealthy
for his class, and he was already engaged to be married
to the dearest and best girl in the whole country side.
His own choice, the mother was pleased to think, bub
hers also heartily and unreservedly.

And then one unforgettable night an icy hand
clutched at her heart-strings, and she grew faint and
sick with terror and foreboding.  Her son had come
home late from a distant fair, unsteady of gait, flushed
of face, and with a wild light in his eyes that only tco
forcibly reminded her of her dead husband. From that

TRINITY’S “ DEFENCE " EXAMINED.

T is a mark of a people’s fitness to rule themselves
and to handle their own affairs that they should

e able to recognise the critical junctures of their for-
tunes, and to rise to the demands which great occasions
‘make. Judging by the signs one sees, it can hardly be
said that our people, as a whole, have yet recognised
‘the crisis of our affairs which is now close at hand. The
THome Government question and the University ques-
‘tion are neither of them receiving their proper share
of thought. To many the University question may
seem wearisomely sfale; yet that question is now com-
ing to.a crisis, and some of us, certainly, feel that it is
“ now or never ”’ with our chances of a settlement. To
1me this seems reason enough for once more approaching

THE LEADER. 71

the question. . Much is at stake. A settlement 1s
within reach, if we will but fight for it; but fight we
musb if we want to convince the Cabinet that we are in
carnest in our demands. Trinity College is_“ defepd-
ing ”’ itself, and in the process of defence it is hurling
out aspersion and insulf, and using to fzhe full its
wrongfully-gotten “ prestige ’—such as it is.  If we,
on our part, remain silent, and fail to give the Trinity
arguments and perversions their proper answer, we can-
1ok escape the appearance of letting our case go by
default, and of either not wanting any University or
not wanting the settlement offered us. That this may,
nob seem so, I propose to examine the statement of the
Trinity College Defonce Committee recently published.

1. In paragraph 1 it is stated that “ Trinity College
is to lose the power to confer degrees.”’ Trinity, as a
College, possesses no such power; it is Dublin Univer-
sity which possesses it. True, it is said that Trinity
and the University are “identical.”” Quite so; aad
that is the fatal blot on Trinity—viz., that there is no
independent body of Examiners to examine for, and of
Senators to confer, degrees.

2. In the same paragraph we are told that the
Governing Body of the reformed University  will con-
duct the University examinations.”” I do not think so.
The Governing Body will appoint the examiners, no
doubt, but surely will not “ conduct the examinations.””

3. “We are entitled to ask upon what grounds of
complaint the constitution of the University is to be
thus overturned.””  Thus opens paragraph 2.  The
grounds of complaint are many, but they are crystal-
lised in the King’s words, that the University needs to
be brought into harmony with modern requirements.
Amongst other grounds are these: that Trinity College
has neglected Irish learning, Irish history and archzo-
logy, and has proved itself no leader of national
thought, either political, sociai, or economic in Ireland.

4. “The Royal Commission has found an emphatic
verdiet in favour of Trinity College as she exists at
present.”” If this means that the Commission found a
verdict in favour of the College continuing on its pre-
sent basis, then it is a false statement, as the Royal
Commission did not favour the present preposterous
system of Senior Fellowships. Moreover, the Fellows
and Professors who connected themselves with the
“Bonn '’ University scheme did, by their very action,
implicitly condemn Trinity “ as she exists at present.”

9. Paragraph 2 of the Defence states that the Bryce
scheme would extinguish “ the autonomy of Trinity Col-
lege.”” It would not as a College, it would continue to
govern itself, and administer its- own affairs and
revenues.

6. Paragraph 3 opens by recognising that the Com-
mission has found that Trinity College is' not adequate
to the requirements of Catholics in respect of higher
education.  Yet the remainder of the paragraph is
practically a defence of Trinity against this verdict of
the very Commission which it declares, in the previous
paragraph, to have pronounced emphatically «in favour
of Trinity College as she exists at present!’”

7. In the course of paragraph 3 it is stated that the
disparity between Protestants and Catholics on the
Governing Body of the present University “ could only
be rectified by the spontancous action of the Roman
Catholic community.” Mark that word only, and then
remember that those who put forward the “Bonn
scheme offered Catholics 25 per cent. of the seats on the
Governing Body. Evidently they thought that ¢the
spontaneous action of the Roman Catholic community
was not the only way of rectifying the disparity in
question,

8. In paragraph 4 there is a reference to the “ pre-
sent independent and unsectarian character ’ of T.C.D.,
and the University of Dublin. Well, they may be “in-
dependent and unsectarian . in ‘their paper constitu-
tion ;  they are nothing of the kind in their personal
constitution, administration, and atmosphere, while
their Divinity Faculty is Protestantly sectarian.

9. In the fifth paragraph occurs the following :—“The
experience of the Primary and Intermediate systems of
education has proved that even a system which dispenses

. With tests, and is in name undenominational, will,
- under the control of the artificially-balanced Board,

o necessarily be managed on denominational lines.”” Not
. to any greater degree than Trinity College is at pre-
senb. Even its alleged “ unsectarian’ character has

only been acquired under the pressure of Catholic agita-
tion, and of the movement of public opinion consequent
thereon.  Trinity never spontancously changed any-
thing of its original character, As to the reference to
‘the Intermediate Board : that body was originally seven

this tragedy for a moment from the past.

Near Mullingar, in the year 1846, lived, in evil odour
I am assured, a certain Sir Francis Hopkins. Je¢
mortuis nil nisi bonum holds good, I suppose, even of
Irish landlords.  But the counsel is an embarrassing
one when those who knew the dead remember nothing
good of them. IHowever, let us say no more than that
he was of his class as they were then. Those were the
days before Parliamentary agitation, whatever its short-
comings otherwise, had secured ‘the three F.’s’’ for
the farmers of Ireland; when the tillers of the soil were
tenants at will, and the landlords had a power almost of
life and death over the serfs—for they were little more—
who slaved to maintain them in wasteful and wicked
idleness.  One night as Sir Francis rode home from
dinner at a neighbouring landlord’s house he was shot
at from behind a ditch. The shot did not reach its
mark; he rode on unharmed after seeing, as he said,
the assailant make off across the fields. A rude ballad
commemorative of the affair says he was ‘ after dining
and partaking of good cheer,’”” so we may conclude that
he saw what he wanted to see rather than what was to
be seen. An information was sworn against one of his
tenants, Bryan Seery by name, a most upright and
respectworthy man. At the trial a clear alib: was sworn
to by many absolutely trustworthy witnesses, against
whose testimony there was only the word of the alarmed
and convivial landlord riding home in the darkness. In
those days attempted murder was punishable by death,
and, incredible as it sounds now, that penalty was
awarded to Bryan Seery. A jury composed of land-
lords and their hangers-on found him guilty in the teeth
of overwhelming evidence of his innocence.” It is a mis-
use of words to call it a judicial scandal. It was a
tragedy of infamous injustice. The whole county was
convinced of Bryan Seery’s innacence, the Bishop and
clergy moved heaven and earth to save him, but British
law—administered by landlords for landlords—sent him
to the gallows because a landlord had heen shot at by
someone else. Shot at, mark you—not killed. In the
presence of a great concourse of people, who knew they
were witnessing a shameful crime, Bryan Seery was
hanged outside Mullingar Jail. ~ When his body was
cub down it was, following the custom then, handed over
to his people. The burial took place in Castletown-
Geoghegan, and I have been told the procession accom-
panying it strefched for miles along the road, so many
thousands came far and near to do him honour. British
law destroyed him as a malefactor; his own regarded
him as a victim and a martyr.

_Years afterwards a wretched .man, another tenant of
Sir Francis Hopkins, confessed to Bishop Cantwell that
he it was who fired the shot that brought poor Seery to
the gallows. The Bishop, in spite of the horror with
which he naturally regarded a creature whose cowardl
silence had cost an innocent man his life, bade him
bogone to America; the harm already done was irrepar-
able. There was no need to hang or imprison another
man to clear Bryan Seery’s memory. Iis own people
who alone counted, had always known him to bé
innocent.

That was sixty years ago—in the good old times!
One wonders now, hearing the story from the lips of
those who were actors in it, how People could have
patiently suffered so great an infamy. They knew a

kind, good husband and father was being hurried out of
Af,he' world by a shameful death for a minor wrong of
which he was innocent; they knew, to use their own
phrase, that ‘“ he had neither hand, act or part in it,”’
yet they looked on at his execution and made no attempt
at rescue. What anguish must his have been when he
looked out over the sea of faces, knowing that among
them there was perhaps the face of him whose offence
he was about to expiate! Yet he died with forgiveness
on In.s lips. To the last he protested his innocence, and
to his confessor he spoke his pardon of the unhappy
wretch for whom he was about to suffer. And they who
did no more than murmur unavailingly when one of
them was done to death without cause—let us not judge
them too “harshly.  There was the line of soldiery

to keep his memory green—and that was all. No, let
me be just to them. They did more. They succoured
his widow and children, and they cherished hatred of
the alien rule that made such cruel injustice possible.
Sixty years after Bryan Seery perished on the scaffold
his wife, an old woman nearing her century of years,
passed away in the very town wherein he suffered. She
had been tenderly cared for, and, if her passage to the
grave brought together a concourse of people small in
comparison with that vast assembly of sixty years ago,
yet the end was marked with honour and respect. The
light of Heaven to him and to her! And to us an
appreciation of what is good in the new times as well as
a sense of what was evil in the old, with a hope and a
